# UC-23.1.6 · Mobile App Crash Rate and User Impact

## Description

Every app crash is a customer experience failure that may lead to churn. This use case tracks crash rates by app version, device, and OS — correlating crashes with user retention and app store ratings. Product managers see which crashes affect the most users and can prioritise fixes based on business impact rather than technical severity alone.

## Value

Every app crash is a customer experience failure that may lead to churn. This use case tracks crash rates by app version, device, and OS — correlating crashes with user retention and app store ratings. Product managers see which crashes affect the most users and can prioritise fixes based on business impact rather than technical severity alone.

## Implementation

(1) Instrument your mobile app to send crash reports and session start events via HEC; (2) include app version, OS version, device model, and user ID; (3) alert when crash rate for any version exceeds 2%; (4) track crash rate trends after new releases; (5) correlate crash-affected users with churn data to quantify business impact.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: HEC (custom app telemetry).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=app_events` `sourcetype="app:crash"` (app_version, os_version, device_model, user_id, crash_type, stack_trace).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Instrument your mobile app to send crash reports and session start events via HEC; (2) include app version, OS version, device model, and user ID; (3) alert when crash rate for any version exceeds 2%; (4) track crash rate trends after new releases; (5) correlate crash-affected users with churn data to quantify business impact.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=app_events sourcetype IN ("app:crash","app:session") earliest=-7d
| eval is_crash=if(sourcetype="app:crash", 1, 0)
| eval is_session=if(sourcetype="app:session", 1, 0)
| stats sum(is_crash) as crashes, sum(is_session) as sessions, dc(user_id) as affected_users by app_version
| eval crash_rate=round(100*crashes/sessions, 2)
| eval users_pct=round(100*affected_users/sessions, 2)
| sort - crash_rate
| table app_version, sessions, crashes, crash_rate, affected_users, users_pct
```

Understanding this SPL

**Mobile App Crash Rate and User Impact** — Every app crash is a customer experience failure that may lead to churn. This use case tracks crash rates by app version, device, and OS — correlating crashes with user retention and app store ratings. Product managers see which crashes affect the most users and can prioritise fixes based on business impact rather than technical severity alone.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=app_events` `sourcetype="app:crash"` (app_version, os_version, device_model, user_id, crash_type, stack_trace). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): HEC (custom app telemetry). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: app_events.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=app_events, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **is_crash** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **is_session** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by app_version** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for comparisons across those breakdowns for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **crash_rate** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **users_pct** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• Pipeline stage (see **Mobile App Crash Rate and User Impact**): table app_version, sessions, crashes, crash_rate, affected_users, users_pct


Step 3 — Validate
Compare crash counts and affected users to your mobile analytics or crash tool (Firebase, New Relic Mobile, vendor SDK) for the same app build and date range. Confirm version and OS filters match how engineering triages.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (crash rate by version over time), Bar chart (crashes by device/OS), Single value (current crash-free rate %), Table (top crash types with user impact).

## SPL

```spl
index=app_events sourcetype IN ("app:crash","app:session") earliest=-7d
| eval is_crash=if(sourcetype="app:crash", 1, 0)
| eval is_session=if(sourcetype="app:session", 1, 0)
| stats sum(is_crash) as crashes, sum(is_session) as sessions, dc(user_id) as affected_users by app_version
| eval crash_rate=round(100*crashes/sessions, 2)
| eval users_pct=round(100*affected_users/sessions, 2)
| sort - crash_rate
| table app_version, sessions, crashes, crash_rate, affected_users, users_pct
```

## Visualization

Line chart (crash rate by version over time), Bar chart (crashes by device/OS), Single value (current crash-free rate %), Table (top crash types with user impact).
